topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias sat by the window, the afternoon light catching the dust motes dancing in the air, a familiar, dull ache settling in his chest. For thirty years, woodworking had been his sanctuary; the scent of aged cedar and linseed oil was the only thing that quieted the incessant, low thrum of worry that had become his constant companion these past few months. Then came Leo’s enthusiasm. Leo, all sharp elbows and boundless energy, suddenly demanded to help, not just observe. He wanted to carve. He wanted to build. The meticulous, calming rhythm Elias had cultivated dissolved into a frantic, loud energy. Every time Leo brought a half-finished, clumsy project to his workshop, Elias felt a tightening in his throat, a profound sense of being overwhelmed by the sheer volume of demand. He kept his hands stiff, avoiding the smooth grain of the maple, feeling utterly depleted, watching his son’s bright, consuming focus overshadow the quiet satisfaction he once found in his own creations.

[story 2]

I traced the chipped paint on the windowsill, the silence of the apartment amplifying the hollow feeling inside me. My old passion—restoring antique clocks—used to be a gentle meditation, a slow unfolding of intricate mechanisms. Now, it was Leo’s domain. He didn't just want to look; he wanted to dismantle, to understand the tiny gears, his brow furrowed in that intense, almost feverish concentration that always made my stomach clench. I remember the day he presented me with a disassembled grandfather clock, his eyes shining with an almost alarming excitement. I nodded, trying to project calm, but inside, a heavy weight pressed down. I found myself hesitating before touching the delicate brass components, the familiar gleam suddenly feeling like an accusation. The sheer intensity of his devotion to the mechanics was exhausting; it left me feeling adrift, watching my quiet world get completely rearranged by his magnificent, demanding obsession.

[story 3]

The workshop smelled overwhelmingly of sawdust and something metallic, a sharp, insistent aroma that grated on my nerves. I leaned against a workbench, my shoulders slumped, watching Leo meticulously sand a miniature wooden bird. It was beautiful, undeniably so, but the speed at which he worked, the relentless precision he applied to every tiny curve, felt aggressive. My own hands felt clumsy and unresponsive, gathering dust on the counter. I used to find solace in the slow, deliberate turning of clockwork escapements, a patient dance of tiny wheels. Now, Leo treated every piece of wood like a puzzle demanding immediate, perfect assembly. When he didn't get the exact alignment right on the first try, his frustration manifested as a sharp intake of breath, and I felt a corresponding drop in my own energy. I just stood there, watching the relentless creation, feeling utterly out of sync, a muted observer in the whirlwind of his perfect, demanding craft.
